item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Blood samples taken from SCP-1166 reveal that it is a genetically-manipulated chimera, with samples containing an abnormally high percentage of human cells (██.█%) in comparison with previously documented non-anomalous specimens.

Implying that normal rats have a nonzero percentage of human cells?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image could not be properly sourced, and is assumed to be non-compliant. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unnecessary censoring of percentages and disease, and I find it more than a little hard to believe the rat would drop random periods and numbers into its words, as opposed to just spelling them wrong.

No vote from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's great. I was afraid, that Ethics Committee response will be nothing more than wordy, polite version of "fuck your whining, we will cut that rat because science". I like the "cold, but not cruel" Foundation, not "let's make them suffer, anaesthetics and amensiacs are for wussies" one. It's a nice middle-ground - nobody's going to hug and cuddle SCP-1166, but also there is no need to make it suffer more than it is necessary.

And it's nice to see that there are decent human beings among researchers, not only emotionless, cynical automatons.

item.title: Re: Wall-o-text incoming
item.content:encoded:
*standing ovation* Your argument has been acknowledged and the issue long since worked out, but I still have to take the time to applaud the amount of thought and effort you put into this. What's more, you were heeded, and the resulting compromise made this article into one that hit me right in the feels, as they say, but not so much that I'd feel the need to write an unhappy essay on the subject myself. You are me, but much better at it and licensed to speak. I worship the ground you walk on, Smapti; I can't proceed without saying it. *continues standing ovation for a good five minutes*

…I don't suppose you could help me write my argument to revive Siggurós (SCP-239)? No? Haha didn't think so. XD I'm just kidding. Seriously, though, even though this happened almost two years ago, you have my eternal respect and admiration, and I will defer to you in all situations from now on. Cheers, Smapti. <3

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: Self-upvoting with staff permission to counteract confirmed case of malicious downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Humans aren't, to the best of my knowledge, immortal mice."

new favorite quote.

-Edit: whoops, I think I replied to the wrong post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the rework about the split decision on the Ethics Committee… And IMO, my headcanon is that the head researcher didn't actually get sent to a new SCP, but rather transferred to the Ethics Committee himself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never saw the original until I read the discussion page, and I must say, this new version is definitely an improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this before you adjusted it, and now like it enough to cancel my downvote.

item.title: You horrible monster
item.content:encoded:
I feel so goddamn sorry for this rat.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better rewrite, canceled downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better. Re-rated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We buried the sentient Australian spider. That's your good and nice. It's sickeningly sweet to have a cute, sentient 1337-speaking rat what has bad stuff happen to it, and then we save it in a PETA-esque fashion through animal-thing rights and all that. I say hurt it more to keep this from being a covert political message to make me save pigs from being turned into pork.

The thing is, from what I'm aware of, the point of the site is congruous to Gears's Razor. It's supposed to make you cringe and feel bad about yourself in some way.

I say if we're gonna be nice to it in the end, you might as well add some test logs depicting us mutilating the thing. Articles with no edge generally don't catch a lot of interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No offense taken at all. In fact, thanks for taking the time and thought on this. This generated FAAAR more feedback than I had anticipated :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an excellent rewrite, and I wish I could upvote it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And downvote now changes to an upvote.

I hope you don't think I was being cruel or picky or anything, Kalinin. I just felt strongly about the Ethics Committee's portrayal and wanted to explain, in detail, why I felt it was OOC. The reaction would seem to suggest that a lot of people hadn't considered it that way, but it's a testament to the overall soundness of the idea, and the empathy you created for Rat Harkness (which I've now decided is its name) that the reaction was "let's fix this" rather than "INTO THE FIREY ORB WITH THEE".

Glad to see this concept turn around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's half the fun of writing for the Foundation.

And I just took this back up to 0 for you. I think you've addressed the concerns here very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good enough. I wouldn't have bent over backwards so far, and fixed just the abject nonsense, but it's good as it stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I've made some changes. Please let me know if this addresses some of the issues people have identified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Points taken. I'll see what I can do to rework this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Side note: I hope I'm not coming off as defensive or anything here. I'll admit to being slightly horrified at thinking I had done a decent job last night, and waking up to find this thing on its way to deletion. Oh, vanity. But I am keen on producing an article worthy of the site and its standards, so I'll try to rework this to keep the core of the story while saving it. On my way to edit now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be a nice meta-nod to the discussion here to have the Ethics Committee divided on the question. I think that's a good idea.

I'm thinking of overhauling the containment procedures to include clearance from the committee before approval of specific tests. This way, it preserves some of the emotional impact of the article, in that they may still be doing distressing things, but the fact that it's a difficult decision in each case is acknowledged.

I don't see why amnesiacs wouldn't work on 1166, since it's a human analogue in terms of drugs etc. I may include that as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see testing making sense in that we want to study 1166 itself, rather than using it as a guines pig. Still, we'd want to make sure we're considering its state of mind (hell, we might even be able to win it over if we can convince it we want to help), and any researchers involved in the project should be fully aware that it's sapient and feels pain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that would be a good compromise position and in character for the Foundation.

I'll just throw it out there as an additional idea, but maybe note that the decision wasn't unanimous and may be submitted for review again after 6 months?

Also, do amnesiacs work on SCP-1166? If so, it might be a good idea to require their usage to reduce long term stress from experimentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All right. I'm thinking over my edits here. One problem universally seems to be the overreaction of the EC. I'm planning on revising their note to have the Researcher transferred to other projects without the mindwipe. For the record, I wrote the Researcher's complaint as one of two generally acceptable viewpoints; his was the fact that the Foundation was being too cruel to a thinking, feeling life form. The Foundation sees it as a valuable asset, and thus will want to continue finding out more about it.

So, my planned changes: update the EC's response, focus the article more on "this SCP has qualities that need to be researched for possible benefits" rather than as a substitute for D-Class testing, and add something in the containment procedures about researchers being interviewed before working on 1166 and being allowed to opt out. That I think would take some of the over the top nastiness that's currently being attributed to the foundation.

How does that strike everyone?

Smapti: I'll reply here since there's threads all over the place. This wasn't intended to be a piece that simply is trying to make the Foundation pointlessly mean. It was meant to be more of a conundrum of "beneficial research to humanity" v. "suffering of the innocent". I think the execution probably needs work to make that come across, but my intentions I think are a little different from your characterization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I suppose the main point of disagreement then is about the conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you notice, I addressed my problems with the note elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But as Smapti and I have pointed out, the Foundation doesn't actually get much benefit from using this thing. Moreover, the Ethic's Committee's response is still unacceptable, even if you do agree with the ultimate conclusion to continue testing on 1166.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In that case, I'd downvote the hell out of it for the ethics committee being a bunch of head-in-the-clouds morons, getting in the way of research that could potentially save lives and resources.
Once again. We do what we can, because we must. The Foundation hasn't got the luxury of fussing about sapient rats, when we're grasping at straws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, this article could be fixed if the order of the last two addenda were reversed - the scientists want to keep testing the rat over and over again because "think of the benefits", and the Ethics Committee says "No, it's not worth it". It would be much more in character as opposed to now where the Ethics Committee is being, well, unethical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are a genuinely horrible person. You know that, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hahaha.
This was accidentally in chat, and it's just as funny now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not downvoting because this is actually completely salvageable with some minor edits. At the very LEAST the rat should be rendered brain-dead before any tests began.

I can dig the idea here, though. It's really just that last bit dragging it down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a simple solution for both the ethical problems of using a non-Foundation sentient and the rat not being able to die. Give him SCP-963.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've written what I would have liked to have said after reading the massive thread up above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes,
In my headcanon, D-class are recruited from death row all over the world with some bullshit about governemnt testing and walking off free if they last for a month, to ensure compliance. Each month , those that are usable for further testing get amnesiac'd and moved to a different facility, under the guise of monthly terminations. In cases of need, D-class are recruited from general prison population, easily missed people, and finally ,general population, in this order, with fabricated false histories.

There, you have it black on white.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I can add to the discussion that I don't feel has already been said is that 1166 is valuable because in some situations, it can stand in for a D-Class. I.e., an SCP of some sort emits unknown types of radiation, we don't know what kind of effects it will have on a human, send in 1166. The fact that 1166 can do this multiple times means it will literally save lives.

"Some sort of radiation, we don't know what it will do to a human" is exactly what we do have D-class for, and the utility of sending in 1166 is questionable even in that circumstance. 1166 can't tell us what it's feeling or experiencing as a result of exposure like a D-class can, especially if we've forbidden the staff from communicating with it. If we want to just test physical reactions to unknown energy and we're suddenly squeamish about using a D-class, as I mentioned in my report, the Foundation has the ability using actual science that exists in the real world to produce human-rat chimeras that would make just as good test subjects as 1166, and which are neither sapient or immortal, thereby eliminating the moral concerns those issues pose.

In the end, it's really just a ham-fisted attempt to force a "message" about how awful the Foundation is into an article that would be better without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For one, his lookout at the way D-class personnel are recruited , and why is it done that way is ,frankly, absurd.

I'm replying that his view point is no more absurd than the Foundation absconding with thousands of prisoners every year to run tests on.

You can accurately accuse me of hyperbole, but not of constructing a strawman. Without outlining what alternative you have in mind I can only assume that your head canon is that the Foundation simply *takes* an undisclosed number of people every year to replace losses.

A D-Class recycling plan would cut the losses down from a staggering amount to something reasonable, which would make volunteer recruitment more manageable as well which could increase the quality of work the D-Class perform since they would be more motivated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. In my head canon, the Foundation would use this rat to save lives, just not D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*sigh*
Sorry.. probably read more into it than I should have. Or less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Either you can read my post as supporting yours, or you can read it as some sort of bizarre contradiction of yours without actually contradicting anything you wrote.

Which of those two options do you think was more likely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This.
Thank you, Anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Either they reuse D-Class across months are there are far too many from certain demographics used in tales and entries.

Of course I think they get mindwiped and reused. That's what my above post asserted, duh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except I never argued that's the case, so please, take your strawman elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually downvoted this when it was at +17, but now canceled my vote, because this is, while dumb in some places, still better than a lot of old articles from the first block that linger at +5 for years.

I am okay with torturing and killing the rat repeatedly in order to protect humanity. I am not okay with torturing and killing the rat repeatedly in order to save a D-class lives. Because I am okay with torturing and killing D-calss repeatedly in order to protect humanity. Figuring out why the rat resurrects counts as protecting humanity, so ultimately I agree with EC's decision, just not with the reasons behind that decision.

The researcher shouldn't be demoted or mind-wiped, just transferred.

item.title: Re: Wall-o-text incoming
item.content:encoded:
This. So much this. In fact, this should be placed in the article itself as the actual answer the Ethics Committee sent back, and then I'd upvote. Because this right here is in character according to what the Tale introducing the Committee showed.

What's currently in the article? It's not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are about two thousand people reportedly sentenced to death a year across the globe outside of China1.

The US itself only sentences something like 100 people a year, with the middle-east, south-east Asia, and Africa having the bulk of the reported death sentences.

Either they reuse D-Class across months are there are far too many from certain demographics used in tales and entries.

Footnotes
1. China doesn't reveal its statistics, but it's expected to be somewhere in the thousands

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's no more absurd than the idea that the foundation empties the equivalent of a small country every year to have disposable bodies to throw at things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are they?
A lot of people's headcanon is that they get amnesiac'd and transported to a different facility. Monthly terminations are a multi-purpose illusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to point out that 1166 standing in for D-Class doesn't really save them, as they'll all get terminated at the end of the month anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly my thought. In fact, through the entire article, I was thinking "but what good is having only one?"

If there were more of them, that'd be another story, but, as things are, there aren't. A sample size of exactly 1 is just as useful as a sample size of exactly 0 if you can't make that number go up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

~EDIT~ Actually, removing my upvote, because the ethics committee thing really bothers me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rat: 1
Ethics committee: 0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the ethics comments. This violates precedent.
Rats die on a regular basis. Plenty of data to gather there. A new death every few years, with plenty of data.

If it must be killed intentionally, at least provide an amnesiac.

Actually, for a creature like this, it might even be a mercy to kill it every time it suffers seriously from old age. You could ask it!

I like this enough to want to prevent its deletion, but there's still plenty of room to fix things. Temporary upvote to buy more time for edits.

item.title: Re: Wall-o-text incoming
item.content:encoded: I definitely agree. As I said, there's next to no actual information you can learn from this thing, and Smapti raises some good points about how the Ethics Committee's treatment of the researcher is utterly out of hand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Be glad, man. You wrote the new SCP-579 in terms of being controversial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I go to bed and then wake up to a shitstorm. Funny.

This all puts me in a quandary. I'm willing to do edits aplenty to tighten up writing, and I'm willing to clean up some of the observed inconsistencies in the Committee's memo. But I'm not sure I'm willing to do edits to change content in order to appease one side (and it appears there are two sides) of an argument.

The only thing I can add to the discussion that I don't feel has already been said is that 1166 is valuable because in some situations, it can stand in for a D-Class. I.e., an SCP of some sort emits unknown types of radiation, we don't know what kind of effects it will have on a human, send in 1166. The fact that 1166 can do this multiple times means it will literally save lives.

I feel okay changing the stuff that VAElynx and others have said about the inconsistencies in the last report. I'm interested in knowing how many more downvotes are over shitty writing (there were a few others about some of the dialogue in the interview log). But this looks a lot like an argument over canon.

Also, I did not do this explicitly to make the Foundation look as horrible as possible. I in fact would have probably reached the same decision the Committee did. But 1166 was an exercise in going to bad places in order to keep protecting humanity, which I felt an appropriate theme for here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Smapti's rebuttal is precisely that. It tries to force a set of motives on the Foundation that it gives little damn about.
For one, his lookout at the way D-class personnel are recruited , and why is it done that way is ,frankly, absurd.
